text.title: Product Launch Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Six launch failures that produce weak or chaotic introductions to market.

antipattern.description: Planning starts the week before launch day.
antipattern.reason: One week is not enough to build a pre-launch audience, create and review assets, coordinate partners, or set up monitoring. Minimum: 6 weeks for a meaningful launch, 8 weeks for a high-impact one.
Friday launches lose the Monday-Tuesday news cycle. Most journalists and influencers are not covering tech news Friday afternoon. Tuesday-Wednesday gives the full week's coverage window.

antipattern.description: Same message posted verbatim to every channel.
antipattern.reason: Twitter audiences, email subscribers, and LinkedIn followers have different contexts and norms. Copy-pasting produces content that feels wrong in each channel and reduces engagement in all of them.

antipattern.description: No pre-launch audience — the product is announced to a cold start.
antipattern.reason: Without an email list, waitlist, or community following, Day 1 signups depend entirely on algorithm and press pickup — both unreliable. Building even 500 subscribers before launch transforms the baseline metric.

antipattern.description: No monitoring on launch day — the team is focused on announcements, not product health.
antipattern.reason: Launch day is peak load and peak visibility for bugs. Server errors and broken flows during the announcement window permanently affect conversion. Assign a dedicated on-call engineer before posting anything.

Launch learnings are the highest-value data point for the next launch. What channel outperformed, which asset under-converted, what the team would change — all disappear if not captured within 2 weeks.
